speaker: interviewee
text: When we were ready-- after my mother had been taken by the Germans, we were trying to find out, of course, where they had taken her. But the only thing that the chauffeur could say was that they were going to be taken to Copenhagen. So we had our friends that was trying, the Madsens, to see if they could find out through some of their family in Copenhagen what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said that Minnie was going to be taken to Germany, and that was all we knew. Then my father had called from Copenhagen and said, I'm ready. How am I going to get there? So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we told my father to take the train from Copenhagen to Helsingor, and of course disguise himself a little bit to not look like everybody else, and just look like the average Joe citizen, you know, this-- doing some business, have a little valise, and so on and so forth. So my father took the train, and nobody bothered asking him any questions or anything. He had bought a ticket, and he went on the train and came to the city of Helsingor. Like I say, that is 12 miles-- or 12 kilometers, which is about eight miles from our city of Hornbaek. So we figured, how are we going to get him up here? So somebody suggested that I go on bicycle, because I was the least suspicious character. So I went to my friend. There was-- his father was a blacksmith in our-- in the summer town of Hornbaek.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I borrowed my friend's clothes, blacksmith clothes what's is all greasy, oily, dirty clothes, and I had a blacksmith hat on. So I took the one bicycle and rode with another one next to me. And I bicycled the eight miles first, into the city of Helsingor. And there my father was, coming up at the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told my father, I said, can you ride the bicycle? Because I had never seen my father on a bicycle. But my father was very agile, and he had had a store repairing bicycles. And he had a motorcycle factory and all this in the old days, before I was born. So he just said, sure, I can ride the bicycle. And he went on the bicycle. And now that I didn't have to have the second bicycle, we went all the way across the back roads, which made it even longer than the eight miles. I'll say maybe we drove 10 or 12 miles on bicycles. And at that time, my father was already 62 years old, but he did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we drove all the way up to the-- back to the city of Hornbaek and in to the Madsens. And that was on the 4th of October that he came home. So he stayed with us. And then we were told that the next day, it looks very good, on the 5th. And so like I say, we went to this beach area, where we were taken down by some already that was helping. And we sat in those big clay pipes down on the beach. Now, other Jews had come there, which I only remember the names of one. He was a great musician called Max Skalka. And he came with a whole suitcase full of all his life's work, you know, and music he has written and all. And my father said to him, you can't take that. That weighs just as much as a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people that helped us down said, we will store it. So don't worry. One day you'll get it back. And of course, he didn't know if he ever would get it back and whatever. But now in time that we were sitting down on the beach, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was cold. It was raining. And there was a little bit of wind, but not that much. So at 10 o'clock, we heard this fishing schooner come, like you hear it-- tuck, tuck, tuck, tuck. And then it stopped. And then there was a rowboat down on the beach. And the fishing-- one of the helpers on the fishing boat came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked around and he said, hey, you're way too many. We can only take half of you. So they took mostly women and children and family that belonged together. And he said, I'll be back. And then he sailed out. And at 2 o'clock in the morning, we heard in the distance the chucking of this fishing boat. And we knew that now it was soon time. And I would just-- I could hear my own teeth, I mean, going pretty bad, because that was how cold it was. And so here we were, and now we knew it was time. And we were-- I think there was nine of us left. And among them was my father Herman Lerno, my brother Paul, my friend Ralph, and myself and a few other people. And all of sudden, we just scooted very quick down to where the boat were. And we jumped in and waded out to where the fishing boat were, and we got on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I saw this boat, I figured, how in the world is he making it across the Strait of Oresund? Because it was a-- a rather small fishing boat. It was not one of the big ones which we normally saw down in the harbor. It was like a little-- little size.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I didn't want to go down into the fishing boat. So I stood up on the side of it and held on to the side. And then he turned the fishing schooner around and started going across the strait. And then we hear it, heard away across, we saw lights coming from-- like from both sides. And we were out in the middle of the open ocean. And he was steering up, north first, and then the boat going a little faster across, because the current was so strong, and he kept going up north. And then it-- it scooted down south. Then he went up north, and then we went back south again. And it was choppy seas now. And if I swore to myself, I said, I will never sail again, because I have never gotten that sick, and not-- I can say it honestly, not thrown up, because I have not done that since I was five years old, I think. And there I was. My brother was sick. My father always looked like it. He was a giant. You know, to me, even, he was no taller than I were, but he was-- because nothing ever phased him as such. From that time when he escaped out of Russia, I guess he had been through it, you know. And so here we were. Now, finally, we see the first one that's coming against us is a Swedish patrol boat, a Swedish destroyer that's going in behind us into the open sea. And he's just very slowly going that way. And so we had time to get into Swedish territorial water. And we could see when we were in Swedish territorial water that the Swedish destroyer had turned around and was now going south. And at the same time, a big German patrol boat was coming from the north, going down south. And of course, they were too late for anything. So-- and they never confronted, really, the Swedish navy. So we chuckled for another hour, you know, until we finally hit the Swedish town called Hoganas. And there we came into the harbor. And and the Swedish police was there, and we were led up to a high school, where at the-- in the gym was laid out mattresses and all. And then they told us the next morning we had to give name and address, and so on. But we were so wet and so cold. And finally, the next day, we-- we got some dry clothes. And then they helped us. They said, if you have family in Sweden, then you should call them and see if they can help you get into the town or someplace. Otherwise, you will be in, like, in the military camp until we can place you around in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had my sister's, sister Ida. She was married to Joel Golding, which had a sister that live in Gothenburg, Sweden. Her name was Esther [? Garelic. ?] And was married to Sam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we called them and asked if they could-- if they would give their name to the police, and if we could use their name, then we would get train tickets to come to Gothenburg. And so we told them that we will call again and let them know when the train was leaving, et cetera. And they picked us up, and we walked then, afterwards, to their house. And when we got there, there was already at-- the apartment was full, it was like we were Norwegian sardines in a can. That was how many people there were. We were eating in shifts, because we couldn't all eat at the same time. There was not room for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right away, we all tried to see if we could find, and then my brother and my friend left us and went to his family, which has also come over to Sweden. So it's just my brother Paul and I. We got one room in an apartment that was rented for us, and my father got another room in another place that was rented for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then we-- we got some money. And then, of course, we started right away looking for work. And my brother got a job, and I got a job, and my father got a job. Even though not so many-- my father was more or less like a helper, but it all helped, you know, to sustain ourselves, you know. And even though-- and we got help from the Swedish government, I must say. They were very nice to us, you know, and helped us with a lot of things. And we could buy clothes at a very reasonable price that they set for-- for us new immigrants into Sweden. And so this is in 1943. So things were going pretty good. Of course, we were told to make packages to the Swedish Red Cross. They would try and find out where my mother was. And they found out that she was in a place called Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia. And if we made Red Cross packages, then they would get it. We made almost every two weeks that we make packages up. And we got cans and pot flowers, you know, and we put medication into the flower. We open up the packages so it couldn't be seen and glued them together again. But later when we asked my mother, she said sometimes she got a little bit, but-- she got some potato flour one time and some other things. But all the chocolate and candies and things we thought that would help them a little bit in surviving, you know, never arrived. We knew why, because the Germans took it, you know, confiscated it, whatever they did, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I heard that there was a Free Danish army being built up in Sweden. Those days it was just called a police force. But this was among my friends. There was a couple of other school friends that have come. There was one, his name was Per Eliasson. And there was another one, his name was Louis Meyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had met each other. And we went out at night, and we went to the amusement park, Liseberg in Gothenburg and had good times. But we also thought about our friends at home and how hard a time that they went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to the Danish general consulate in Stockholm, Sweden, and said that I would like to volunteer into this police force. They wrote back and said, how old are you? I said 17 and 1/2. They'd said that you had to be 18, and I said I was 17 and 1/2 and so, physically, I'm strong enough to be able to-- to volunteer. So they said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I needed my father's consent. My father thought that that was a great idea, so he said OK. In October 1944, I have gotten a ticket to come to Stockholm. And from Stockholm it was with a train there to go to this camp that we were going to. And, of course, when I got off the bus, you were in-- in mud up to your ankles already. And I said, oh, my nice clothes and all. Then that night, we were just given a place to sleep, you know, a bunk, and that was it. The next day, we were outside. And we got our numbers and papers and everything else. And then we got what they call uniforms, which was not that warm clothes, you know. And And we had rain and sleet, and soon it begin to snow. And so it was-- it was hard training every day. And it was training and training and training. I don't remember anything else, you know, and sleeping and walking or running, and learning fighting and shooting and going to the shooting range. And the food was not the-- it was like-- like real army food, you know. But then, of course, the Swedes, they never ate as good as the Danes. So it was even worse, you felt. And many of us got sick from bad food and everything else. But at least we built up units. And my two other brothers all-- we were four brothers, actually, that wanted to volunteer. But my older brother already had too many kids, so they didn't want him to volunteer. So we were three brothers in the army at the same time, my brother Paul and my brother Harry. But they went different battalions, because they volunteered at different times, after me. I'm the first one in. And they first came in in early '45. And so in April of '45, we knew-- we had heard on the radio that Prince Bernadotte and the Danish Red Cross and Danish offers and buses that was painted white have gone to Germany. The Swedish prince had negotiated with Himmler of-- that all Scandinavian prisoners should be returned to the country of origins. And at that time, Germany had already started falling. So they made a deal with Himmler. And the buses were sent out of Denmark-- that was in April of 1945-- to Germany and into Czechoslovakia. And they came to the camp. And my mother didn't know it, maybe, till an hour before. They wondered what those buses were come into the city for. And they were told to pack all their things, or take the least with them as possible. But boarded the buses, and that was it. My mother took-- she had been working as-- as a maid, as a cleaner woman, as a-- stacking books and all-- and other things from other prisoners that had come into the camp. They had come in, and it was like they was there maybe a couple of days. And then the Germans again sent them on cattle cars. And there was Eastern Europe's Jews, you know, that came in, Italian Jews, wherever they came from, into this camp of Therisienstadt. And from there, they were sent on to Auschwitz, and maybe some to Belsen and other extermination camps. And so there was those two things that my mother had seen in the-- in this room that she had stacked things. There was a prayer book, which was a book that, when it opened, it opened on the page. And inside was two things that was pressed in. It was a four leaf clover and an edelweiss, which was a song, Edelweiss, you know. And my mother took [INAUDIBLE] when she saw that, you know. She said-- she put the [INAUDIBLE] book aside. And then there was a front shawl of a Catholic priest, which she saw was a beautiful brocade. And she had put those aside. And she went-- and she said, I'll be back in a minute, and she went into her workplace. And even though the German was standing outside, she packed up the book and the shawl in a piece of paper, you know, and walked out. And the Germans never said anything to her. And she went up on a bus, and she took this with her. Anyway, she came back-- I will talk about those two things later. But she-- they boarded the bus. And she never really told me how many days it took to drive to Germany, first Czechoslovakia and then Germany, into Hamburg, or around Hamburg, and up to southern Schleswig and then into Denmark, across Denmark, and then they were ferried across to Sweden, because the war was still raging. And my mother said that every so often, they stopped. That was when there was bombings of Germany. And so the buses stopped, you know. And the buses otherwise never stopped. There was two chauffeurs on each bus. So-- and when they were driving, they drove four hours at a time. And then one chauffeur slipped in and back of the other, and the other one took to the right, and then the bus kept driving. And my mother said when they came to Kiel, which of course, from the Kiel Canal was a German port. And my mother said that she could see part of a church tower. Otherwise, she said when she looked to the left when they driving in the bus, you could see from one side of Kiel to the other side, right through it. It was all flat. And then finally, they came up to southern Schleswig, which was at one time part of Denmark, but now is Germany. And she said there, it was like nothing ever had happened there, because it was just farms and all, and they were still there. And when they came into Denmark, they were given flowers and hugs and screams and welcome home, you know. My mother just said she-- she just sat there and cried. And then the buses went across Sweden. I had gone to my commander and said, I know that some-- a train is coming up to Stockholm on the 17th. He said, we are on ready alert. But he said, I'll tell you, I'll give you the trip into Stockholm. And you see the train, and then you come right back. And I said, thank you, you know, and I left. And I went to Stockholm. Unfortunately, there was two columns of trains. One went up to Stockholm, and the other column went to a city called Helsingborg. And my mother, unfortunately, was on the other train. But I saw an old girlfriend that had also been interned that came up. And I-- it was her, Kate. [? Hofgorn ?] was her name. Later, she married a friend of mine. And I-- I asked her if she knew where my mother was. She said, I think she is on the other train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I had no more reason to-- to stay there. I saw the Rabbi Friediger. He had also been interned, because he never believe that the Germans would come to the house. And so at the Seder one night, they came and just knocked on the door. And he went and opened the door, and they just left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, they took them, you know? So then I had no other reason to stay, that I knew that they were free now. And I went back to camp the same day. And I went to the captain and I thanked him for letting me. So he said, I want you to be in charge of all ammunition. And there will be a column of trucks, you know, and you will ride front gun on the truck, you know, and I said, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we went to the southern part of Sweden and were lying there in camp. We were lying in tents. And we slept-- I think there was 24 in each tent, you know, and had like a little stove in the middle, which didn't do nothing. So for two weeks we were lying there freezing our rear ends off. And unfortunately, the Germans in Germany capitulated to the British on the 4th of May at 8 o'clock at night. But nobody knew it in Denmark till the 5th of May. And on the 5th of May, we were moving to the harbor in Elsinore, and we sailed across to Denmark. We thought that we were going to be greeted by Germans' guns coming across the strait. And so we were all in-- in full gear, lying on the bottom of the-- on the decks, you know, with our guns ready and all, ready to go aboard to Denmark. And instead, we were shouted, welcome home, about the 10,000 people in the city of Elsinore. It was such a beautiful sight that you can't-- we have the Hamlet castle on the right and the-- the beaches on the left, you know, and here sits the harbor. And here was red and white flags put up, you know, and people were-- just started shouting, welcome home. But of course, as a military unit, you can't always do-- so we got flowers, and then we start marching from Elsinore into Copenhagen. And then we were going to overnight at schools around the city. And the school that we came to was called [? Husome ?] Street School. I just took a picture here, when I were in Denmark my last time on the 5th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's funny to see the school again, the backyard where we were lying in and had our open fights with the Germans. We lost three soldiers, because the still Danish quislings and the Danish Nazis were attacking us, you know? And of course, we blew them out of the sky. But we lost three.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were sent the next day. We were already sent to-- down to the border of Denmark and Germany to take over the border guarding. And there we met the British unit which had been fighting before and started a war in Tobruk. So it was a-- an old unit, you know, of British soldiers. And then later came the Americans up too. And so we were called-- when I say it, the Danish Brigade. But we got an American name, because it was all under Eisenhower and Montgomery. So we were called Dan Force. That was-- our military unit was called. And so we've got the border patrol together with the British, and that meant that we now were throwing out all the Germans from Denmark. And this is of course 1945 now. And from-- from the 5th of May to the end of July, when we were sent home, because in that span of time, the Danish military already were functioning that we had-- all our units had come in. And the-- what shall I say-- the directors of the units and all, with the former Danish military, was all called up again. And so new Danish units were called back into service. And in July, they were all ready. And then we relinquished all our weapons, and that was it at that point.
